in the terrible conflagration. A
committee consisting of Byron B.
Defebach, as chairman; Mayor c.
f. Riggs, T. C. Bell, E. d. Farmin
and H. H. Maggie has been named
by the mayor. The committee had
a meeting yesterday morning, and
since that time a canvass has been
partially made to determine what
is most needed in the way of re-
lief AS the fire progresses and
more homes are lost as Is sure to
be the case, the committee will
have a better idea of how enor-
nous is their task.


LOSS AT SA0lE.


The following is list of the
properties lost near Sagle during
the fires:


Oliver Turnbull. storekeeper at
Sagle; store and contents, besides
residence, which was occupied by
his clerk, Mr.. Crumb; stock valued
at $500; partially insured.


w. E. Kinney, $4,000 feet Of
white pine lumber in piles along
the Northern Pacific tracks; also
about 10000 posts.


George Matthews house and out
buildings; orchard ruined


T. P. Craig; house and barn, 10
tons of hay


August Anderson; barn, 20 tons
of hay.


Claude Studebaker; house and
furniture all burned.


Andrew Turnbull; two barns
with hay burned.


Two county bridges, one nearly
200 feet long and the other over
100 feet, were completely destroyed
making traffic impossible.


Frank Leick lost two barns and
nearly so tons of hay.


Tom Longworth lost his barn
and hay.


Nick Pontious lost about $4,000
in posts and poles in the woods.
